ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for cempedak goreng. Mapped to the closest parent class 'fried food product' as the most defensible FoodOn anchor. Cempedak (Artocarpus integer) is a distinct species from jackfruit and has no dedicated FoodOn entry.
ontology.foodex2_description: Jackfruit (used as closest defensible FoodEx2 analogue for cempedak, Artocarpus integer, which lacks a dedicated FoodEx2 code)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for cempedak (Artocarpus integer). Code A0EJW (Jackfruit) is used as the closest taxonomic analogue within the Artocarpus genus. The fried preparation modifier is noted but no specific FoodEx2 process facet is appended to avoid code fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for cempedak goreng or cempedak fruit as a food item. SNOMED CT does not provide adequate coverage for Southeast Asian traditional snack foods at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Cempedak Goreng is approximately 60 g, equivalent to 2–3 battered and deep-fried cempedak segments, as commonly sold at Malaysian and Singaporean roadside stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Cempedak Goreng exists in the HPB Foodo database. Values are adapted from HPB data for deep-fried battered fruit snacks and cempedak fruit composition, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for jackfruit (USDA FDC ID 174687) as the closest approved taxonomic analogue. Saturated fat estimated from typical palm oil frying medium composition per HPB cooking oil data. The input AI-generated values were reviewed against these approved sources and found to be within plausible ranges; they are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for jackfruit / tropical battered fried fruit
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cempedak Goreng in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: battered deep-fried tropical fruit (jackfruit analogue, GI ~55–62 range) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The rice flour batter contributes moderate starch; deep-frying fat slows gastric emptying, moderating the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 19.5) / 100 = 11.3. Carbohydrate per 60 g serving = 32.5 × 0.60 = 19.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (95 mg/100 g) with beneficial potassium (280 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Cempedak Goreng is relatively low in sodium and provides potassium, which supports blood pressure regulation; however, the high caloric density from deep-frying should be considered in the context of overall dietary energy balance for individuals managing hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~58) with moderate sugar content (15.5 g/100 g) from natural fruit sugars and rice flour starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one piece (approximately 20–25 g) and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the combination of natural fruit sugars and rice flour starch can elevate glycemic response despite the fat-mediated moderation of GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (12.8 g/100 g) including saturated fat (~4.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying in palm oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from deep-frying in palm oil contributes to LDL cholesterol risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Cempedak Goreng as an occasional snack and limit consumption to one serving per occasion within a diet low in saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential from deep-frying oils and high sugar content, partially offset by antioxidants (vitamin C 18 mg, vitamin A 45 mcg RAE)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While cempedak provides antioxidants including vitamin C and carotenoids that may support anti-inflammatory pathways, the pro-inflammatory nature of deep-frying oils and high sugar content warrants moderation for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cempedak Goreng is a beloved seasonal Malay and Singaporean street snack made from the deep-fried segments of the cempedak fruit (Artocarpus integer), a tropical tree fruit closely related to jackfruit but distinguished by its more intense, musky, honey-like aroma and creamier flesh. To prepare the snack, individual fruit segments — each encasing a large edible seed — are coated in a light batter typically made from rice flour, turmeric (for its characteristic golden colour), and water or coconut milk for added crispness. The battered segments are deep-fried in hot oil until the exterior is shatteringly crisp and golden, while the interior fruit becomes extraordinarily soft, custardy, and intensely sweet. The seeds, which become nutty and tender upon frying, are also consumed. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 245 kcal, 3.5 g protein, 12.8 g fat (including ~4.2 g saturated fat from frying oil), and 32.5 g carbohydrates with 15.5 g natural sugars and 3.8 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 58 (medium), with a glycemic load of ~11 per 60 g serving. It is a good source of potassium (280 mg) and provides modest amounts of vitamin C (18 mg), vitamin A (45 mcg RAE), and iron (1.2 mg). Clinically, it is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its sugar and starch content, moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from deep-frying, and low concern for hypertension given its low sodium profile. It is naturally gluten-free (rice flour batter), vegetarian, and vegan. Cempedak Goreng is sold at roadside stalls and pasar malam (night markets) in Malaysia and Singapore during the cempedak fruiting season, typically enjoyed as an afternoon tea-time snack.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cempedak goreng?
What is the GI of cempedak goreng?
What is the difference between cempedak and jackfruit?
Where can I find cempedak goreng in Singapore?
What nutrients does cempedak goreng contain?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Cempedak Goreng exists in the HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB deep-fried battered fruit snack data and USDA jackfruit data (FDC ID 174687) as the closest approved taxonomic analogue. The AI-generated input values are within plausible ranges for this food type and are retained with medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using battered deep-fried tropical fruit as the reference food.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): cempedak goreng is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero for this food. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): cempedak fruit and rice flour batter contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central jackfruit entry and HPB plant food data. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero): this is a plant-based vegan food with no cholesterol-containing ingredients; naturally zero. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for deep-fried cempedak goreng is not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation; data unavailable from approved sources. 3. saturated_fat_g (4.2 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (5.1 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.8 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g) are estimated from typical palm oil frying medium fatty acid profile per HPB cooking oil composition data, as no direct measurement for cempedak goreng fatty acid fractions is available in approved sources. 4. GI value of 58 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Cempedak Goreng exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 5. FoodEx2 code A0EJW (Jackfruit) used as closest taxonomic analogue (closest_parent match type); no dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for cempedak (Artocarpus integer). 6. FoodOn mapped to broader 'fried food product' class (closest_parent match type); no species-specific FoodOn term for cempedak goreng. 7. Dietary tag 'low_sodium' from input is retained as sodium is 95 mg/100 g, which is below the HPB low-sodium threshold of 120 mg/100 g for snack foods.
